details.text: Derivative: انْشَقَّتْ from اِنْشَقَّ – اِنْشِقاق and means to be cracked open or split apart and is used in relation to things which are not normally associated with being split and broken apart. It refers to a breaking that is irreversible.

- Literally means “Behind (وَرَاءَ) his back (ظَهْرِهِ)” and it refers to where his left hand is chained, in the case where it has been said that his right hand will be chained to his neck and his left hand chained behind his back.
Derivative: سَوْفَ is used to indicate to the near future and it can be understood to mean “soon”.

Derivative: ثُبُورًا means هِلَاك (death and destruction) which means here that he will be calling, supplicating or begging for a “final death and ultimate destruction” so that he does not have to face his consequences.
Derivative: سَعِيرًا refers to a towering enraged and blazing fire.
Derivative: يَحُورَ from حَارَ – يَحُورُ – حَوْرًا which means to be returned and implies returning back to where one started i.e., in this context it means to be returned back to life.
Derivative: الشَّفَقِ refers to the redness that appears upon the horizon after sunset.
Derivative: لَا “No” here is a negation of the false ideas or beliefs that may precede and are contrary to the truths that are about to be presented.
Derivative: وَسَقَ means to gather and envelop.
Derivative: اتَّسَقَ means to come together and become full or complete.

Derivative: لَتَرْكَبُنَّ “You will most certainly ride”. The addition of لَام التَوْكِيد (Laam At-Tawkeed - The Laam of emphasis) and the heavier Nun with a Shaddah (نَّ) which is also used for providing emphasis provide the verb تَرْكَبُ “You will ride” the greatest level of emphasis. In addition, the word is originally تَرْكَبونَ (i.e., the male plural “you” form) but has omitted the “Waw” (و) because the meeting of As-Saakinain i.e., two Sukoons. Here, the word to “ride” implies “moving” or “progressing” from one stage after another stage in human life.

Derivative: أَذِنَتْ from أَذِنَ which can have two meanings depending on what Masdar (noun associated with the verb) is intended. If it is the Masdar إِذْن (Idhn) أَذِنَ means to permit. If it is أَذَن (Adhan) it means to listen intently and carefully. Here it means to listen intently and comply. It implies the enthusiastic listening to an instruction in order to comply with it.
Derivative: حُقَّتْ is the passive voice (مَبْنِي عَلَى المَجْهُول) form of حَقَّ and it means “As it was only right and becoming for it to do so” i.e., what other choice has it got except to do so.

Derivative: يُوعُونَ linguistically refers to what “They contain or retain”. The origin is أَوْعَى - إِيعاءً which refers to preserving goods in a container like a bag. Here, it can refer to what they keep hidden inside their hearts or to their concealed intents. It can also refer to what: “They gather together” i.e., in terms of deeds.

Derivative: بَشِّرْ is the command form of بّشَّرَ – تَبْشِير which means “to give tidings” and is usually used for good news and congratulating. Here, it is used in a manner to mock or show scorn towards them.

Derivative: مَمْنُونٍ is from the root of مَنَّ “to cut” which is used for example in the statement مَنَنْتُ الحَبْل “I cut the rope”. مَمْنُونٍ means “to be cut” and غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍ means “not cut” i.e., uninterrupted and continuous.

Derivative: مُدَّتْ is the passive voice (مَبْنِي عَلَى المَجْهُول) form of مَدَّ which means to stretch and expand out and it means to “be stretched out” and in a Hadith it is explained like the stretching out of an animal’s hide or strip of leather i.e., stretched tight whereby it is levelled and smoothed out of creases.
Derivative: تَخَلَّتْ from تَخَلَّى – تَخَلِّي which means to completely empty out.
Derivative: أَلْقَتْ from أَلْقَى – إِلْقاء which means to cast out.

Derivative: كَادِحٌ from كَدَحَ – كَدْحًا is an اِسْم الفَاعِل (Ism ul-Faa’il - Noun of the doer) and as such implies active engagement. كَادِحٌ means to be actively engaged in toiling laboriously i.e., working hard and exerting yourself in labour and efforts. كَدْح was explained by Al-Imam Ash-Shawkaniy to mean: “The striving for something with effort without any difference in respect to that thing being good or bad”.
Derivative: مُلَاقِي from لَاقَى – يُلاقِي which means to meet.
- This can mean either: “You will meet Him i.e., your Rabb (Lord)” or “You will meet it” referring to your كَدْح (Kadh) that which you strove laboriously for i.e., the fruits of your labour.

Derivative: يَمِينِ (Yameen) in addition to meaning the “right hand”, it is also an expression of power, honouring and of agreement in terms of something being a done deal. As such giving the book in the “right hand” implies empowerment, honour and an agreement being seen through to its completion.

Derivative: أُوتِيَ means “to be given” and it is in the past tense. However, the past tense in Arabic is not only used for the past but is rather also used to denote the certainty of the act, where the future is spoken about with the past tense to indicate that it is certain or a done deal that it will happen.
Derivative: مَسْرُورًا (happy and delighted) comes from the word سُرُور which means happiness or delight.
